text: The laparoscopic view shows the repair of a recurrent type IV hiatal hernia following a norovigasic bypass procedure, with surgical instruments manipulating the hernia site.
The patient is a sixty-nine-year-old female with a surgical history of a laparoscopic norovigasic bypass and a hiatal hernia repair done in twelve repaired primarily only.
She presented at an outpatient clinic with epigastric abdominal pain associated with reflux symptoms for three weeks prior to her presentation. This was worsened with food and fluid intake.
Her past medical history is extensive and is listed. Her past surgical history is also significant for a laparoscopic ventricular hernia repair with MASH done in two thousand thirteen.

On physical examination, her BMI was two point three. She lost a net of eighty-three pounds since her gastric bypass in two thousand twelve. Her physical examination was unremarkable. Her chest was clear.
A CT scan of the abdominal pelvis with contrast was ordered in order to further investigate her abdominal pain.
The CT scan reveals herniation of the entire rule limb as well as the gastric pouch in the medias time.
The video shows the herniation of the Roux limb and omentum into the hiatus, clearly visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect and take down adhesions between the omentum and the hiatus, separating these structures.
The importance of traction and contra-traction by the assistant is shown here.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to lyse adhesions between the small bowel and the peritoneal sac, carefully separating the tissues.
The surgeon employs a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to separate tissue layers, likely addressing the herniated structures previously identified.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down adhesions and dissect the posterior aspect, continuing the process of separating tissue layers.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the peritoneal sac, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The importance of traction and contra-traction is shown again here.
The surgeon completes the division of the peritoneal sac, ensuring it is fully separated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon removes the excised peritoneal sac from the Abdallah Kafti, completing the excision process.
A flexible endoscope is inserted, providing a clear view of the gastric pouch, while the surgeon uses the endoscope to assist in taking down posterior adhesions.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect and take down adhesions between the gastric pouch and the pleura, facilitating the mobilization of the gastric pouch into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon re-approximates the Hiatal defect using figure-of-eight sutures with 2-0 Surgidac, visibly closing the defect in the tissue.
The robust tissue is visually observed coming together without tension after surgical manipulation.
The surgeon uses an in-the-stitch suturing device to perform suturing on the surgical site, likely involving the gastric pouch or surrounding tissues.
The surgeon places three figure-of-eight sutures to close the Hyal defect, ensuring the tissue edges are securely approximated.
Following the completion of the posterior cruroplasty, the surgeon places synthetic bio-absorbable mesh to reinforce the repair, ensuring stability and support for the surgical site.
We chose absorbable mesh in order to avoid mesh-related complications associated with permanent mesh.
The surgeon sutures the absorbable mesh to the diaphragm using multiple interrupted 2-0 Surgidec sutures, employing the indoor stitch autosuturing device for precise placement.
Care must be taken not to injure any vital structures while placing these sutures, such as the heart on the left side and the aorta and the IVC on the right side.
The surgeon reinforces the hernia repair by buttressing the repair site with a piece of omentum, adding an additional layer of support to the previously placed mesh.
The surgeon sutures the omentum to the diaphragm using a 2-0 suture with a running stitch technique, securing the omentum in place.
The intraorbital endoscopy reveals a large, deep ulcer within the gastric pouch, with a tablet visible in the crater of the ulcer.
on post-APD number one and shows intra-abdominal location of the gastric pouch and roll-in and normal propagation of contraps.
A clear liquid diet was started on post-APD number one after the upper GI study was performed.
The patient was eventually discharged on post-APD number seven on a full liquid diet, proton pump inhibitor, and sucralafate.
The discharge was somewhat delayed due to initial poor PO intake as the patient was having post-aprandian upper gastric abdominal pain secondary to the gastric ulcer.
On subsequent follow-up, on post-daily number fourteen, the patient had no issues. She had very minimal pain and was tolerating her diet very well. She was advanced to a soft diet subsequently.
